params.text:
Communication Skills in Care

Communication is the foundation of good care. Building trust, keeping people safe, respecting choices and working as a team all depend on communicating well. In this module you'll explore what good communication looks like in a care setting and practise it through short interactive activities.

By the end you will be able to:

  • Describe the communication cycle and the main types of communication
  • Use active-listening techniques with the people you support
  • Recognise and reduce barriers to communication
  • Adapt your communication to individual needs
  • Understand your duty to record and report accurately

The communication cycle

Communication is a two-way process. A sender forms a message and sends it; a receiver takes it in, interprets it, and gives feedback. At every step the message can be strengthened or distorted.

  1. Sender — decides what to communicate and how.
  2. Message — the words, tone, and body language used.
  3. Receiver — hears, sees, and interprets the message.
  4. Feedback — shows whether the message was understood.

Checking feedback — "Have I understood you correctly?" — is what turns talking at someone into communicating with them. Watch how the message flows around the cycle:

params.alt: Animated diagram of the communication cycle: sender, message, receiver and feedback
